name: Partial Redundancy Elimination
summary: Morel and Renvoise (1979): eliminate expressions that are redundant on *some* control-flow paths but not all. Generalises both CSE and LICM. Knoop, Rüthing, Steffen's Lazy Code Motion (1992) refined the placement to minimise register pressure.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Morel and Renvoise (1979) introduced PRE as a generalisation of both CSE and LICM: eliminate expressions that are redundant on *some* control-flow paths but not all. The classical algorithm places computations at points that make them fully redundant at the use site, balancing speedup against code-size increase from additional computations on previously-unaffected paths.

PRE is the compiler-theoretic framework underlying LICM and CSE: both are special cases of PRE where the set of paths is restricted to loops or straight-line code respectively.

sections.title: Lazy Code Motion
sections.content:
**Lazy Code Motion** (Knoop, Rüthing, Steffen 1992) improved on Morel-Renvoise by minimising register pressure — placing computations as late as possible while preserving the partial-redundancy elimination. The classical PRE places computations early to expose more redundancy; LCM observes that placing them at the latest possible point still eliminates the same redundancies but keeps the values' live ranges shorter, reducing spill pressure.

LCM uses four dataflow analyses: anticipability (the expression is needed on every path forward), availability (computed already on every path back), latestness (latest point where placement is safe), and redundancy (whether placement here actually eliminates a redundancy). The algorithm computes these in sequence and places each expression at its latest legal point.

Modern compilers (GCC, LLVM) use variants of Lazy PRE rather than the original Morel-Renvoise formulation. The register-pressure consideration matters more on modern backends with limited register sets and long basic blocks.

sections.title: PRE as an optimisation framework
sections.content:
PRE's framing — eliminate redundancy partial across paths, not just full — generalises naturally to many other optimisations. LICM is PRE restricted to loop boundaries. CSE is PRE restricted to a single basic block. Hoisting and sinking are PRE with placement constraints in one direction or the other.

This framing matters because each individual optimisation can be implemented as a special case of the same dataflow framework. GCC's GIMPLE optimisations use a unified PRE framework for many of these transformations; LLVM keeps them as separate passes for engineering clarity but the underlying dataflow is the same shape.

sources.label: Morel-Renvoise — Global optimization by suppression of partial redundancies
